title: Recording and Playing Macros
subtitle: {key:qa} records into register {key:a}. {key:q} stops. {key:@a} plays.
summary: qa starts recording into register a; press q again to stop. @a plays back. @@ replays the most recent macro.

blocks.text: Macros are Vim's loop. Record a sequence of keystrokes once; replay it with one key. They're how you turn 100 lines of repetitive editing into 4 keystrokes.

While recording, the status line shows `recording @a`. Don't worry — the recording is just keystrokes-into-a-register; nothing is locked, nothing is precious. If you mess up, press {key:q} to stop, then {key:q}{key:a} to start fresh.

blocks.title: How Recording Works
blocks.text:
{key:q}{key:a}: Vim *opens register `a` for writing* and tags every subsequent keypress to be appended to it. Each key still does its normal job — you're editing as usual.

{key:q}: Closes the register. The recorded keystrokes (including all the {key:Esc}, motion, and operator keys) sit in {key:"}{key:a} as a string.

{key:@}{key:a}: Reads {key:"}{key:a} and feeds the contents back into Vim's input buffer. They run as if you typed them. Errors abort playback.

This is **just** *type-then-replay*. There's no "macro language." Anything you can type, you can record.

blocks.title: Idiom: Make Your Macro Idempotent
blocks.text:
A reliable macro starts with a *stable anchor* — usually {key:0} (start of line) or a search. That way, no matter where the cursor is, the macro begins at a known position.

It usually ends with a *forward step* — typically {key:j} or {key:n} — so successive plays move down through the buffer.

Example: "add a semicolon to end of every line." Record: {key:q}{key:a}, {key:A}{key:;}{key:Esc}, {key:j}, {key:0}, {key:q}. Now {key:1}{key:0}{key:0}{key:@}{key:a} adds semicolons to 100 lines.

If the macro fails (e.g. no more lines, or no match for a search), playback aborts. That's why "do it 100 times" is safe — it stops when there's nothing left to do.

blocks.title: Macros are input virtualization
blocks.text: Recording a macro stores the literal keystroke sequence into a register. Playback feeds those keystrokes back through the same input pipeline a real keyboard would feed. Every subsystem — operators, search, marks, insert — must remain callable from synthetic input. That's why macros stop on the first command that would have rung the bell: the input simulator treats a failed command as a real failed command and aborts the stream.
